attributes.stem:
A 73-year-old man feels unwell and faints seven days after he underwent an elective left hemicolectomy for an adenocarcinoma. The procedure was uncomplicated and, until now, he had been making an uneventful recovery.

On examination his temperature is 39.6°C, pulse rate 110/min and blood pressure 86/62 mmHg. His respiratory rate is 16/min, oxygen saturation 96% on room air. On chest examination, there is dullness at the left base. His peripheries are cool and clammy. There is some tenderness and redness over the wound site, but the abdomen is otherwise normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Sepsis is defined as an inflammatory response by the host to an infecting pathogen that may be significantly amplified by endogenous factors. A person is said to have SIRS (systemic inflammatory response syndrome) if they meet two or more of the following criteria:

  • Temperature >38 or <36

  • Heart rate >90/min

  • Respiratory rate >20/min or PaCO2 <32mmHg

  • White blood cell count >12,000/mm3 or <4000/mm3 or >10% immature bands

If hypotension then follows (as in this case), the situation can then be defined as one of severe sepsis. There is now haemodynamic compromise that causes a severe mismatch between the supply and demand of oxygen to body organs and tissues, which presents as low blood pressure, poor perfusion and signs of end organ damage clinically. In such circumstances there will be a release of inflammatory mediators (eg histamine), a marked increase in capillary permeability and a resulting reduction in peripheral vascular resistance. Septic shock is a distributive type of shock.

From the above stem this patient is febrile with tachycardia placing him in the category of SIRS. He is also hypotensive, tachycardic and displaying signs of peripheral shut down (cool and clammy extremities), suggesting severe sepsis is present. This patient is extremely unwell and requires urgent resuscitation and treatment to recover.

There is no chest pain or an ECG with changes suggestive of a myocardial infarction (MI). MI can be a cause of shock if there is loss of enough viable myocardium that results in the heart being unable to effectively pump blood around. This would not explain his fever.

Pulmonary embolus can cause haemodynamic compromise if large enough, he may complain of chest pain and shortness of breath. He is saturating well, and his respiratory rate is within normal range. A low-grade fever may be seen in some cases of pulmonary embolism, however it is rare and even more rare to have a high fever such as the one in this case.

His physical exam findings are consistent with a potential chest infection, such as lobar pneumonia. This may cause shortness of breath, fever, cough and some oxygen requirement. It would not explain his hypotension and tachycardia that is suggestive of shock. It may be that his sepsis has developed from the pneumonia infection.

A deep wound abscess would help to explain his fever and could lead to sepsis and septic shock. However, from the stem there is no suggestion of the wound site looking infected or evidence of an abscess. Mild tenderness and redness would be expected after surgery, but should be monitored carefully. Again, with his hypotension and tachycardia the diagnosis is septic shock.
learningPoints: SIRS is diagnosed when a person has any 2 of: temp >38 or <36, heart rate >90, resp rate >20 or a white cell count >12000
Fever with haemodynamic instability should always raise a suspicion of septic shock
Pulmonary embolus rarely causes a fever, and if it does it is often low grade
references: Stein PD, Afzal A, Henry JW, Villareal CG. Fever in acute pulmonary embolism. Chest. 2000 Jan;117(1):39-42. doi: 10.1378/chest.117.1.39. PMID: 10631196.

Standl T, Annecke T, Cascorbi I, Heller AR, Sabashnikov A, Teske W. The Nomenclature, Definition and Distinction of Types of Shock. Dtsch Arztebl Int. 2018;115(45):757-768. doi:10.3238/arztebl.2018.0757
Singer M, Deutschman CS, Seymour CW, et al. The Third International Consensus Definitions for Sepsis and Septic Shock (Sepsis-3). JAMA. 2016;315(8):801-810. doi:10.1001/jama.2016.0287
